programs.name: Medicaid
programs.what_it_does: Provides healthcare coverage through Rhode Island's publicly-funded state Medicaid program for eligible seniors and disabled individuals. It offers multiple options including long-term services and supports (LTSS).[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income older adults, people with disabilities, and those with chronic care needs.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Comprehensive healthcare including LTSS, home care, and Medicare premium assistance for qualified individuals.[1][3]

programs.name: Rhode Island Community Living and Attendant Services Waiver (CLASS)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers home and community-based services to help seniors and disabled individuals avoid institutionalization. Custom services for those living in community settings rather than nursing homes.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults and people with disabilities or chronic care needs eligible for nursing home level care.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Personal care, home modifications, respite, and other supports to maintain independence in the community.[1]

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Offers comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings, including adult day health care. Integrates Medicare and Medicaid benefits for coordinated care.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors age 55+ who are nursing home eligible but can live safely in the community with services.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of Medicare/Medicaid services plus extras like transportation, meals, and therapies.[1]

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP)
programs.what_it_does: Helps pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, copays, and coinsurance for eligible low-income seniors; includes QMB (full assistance), SLMB (Part B premium), and QI (Part B premium). Also QDWI for working disabled under 65.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Limited income and assets Medicare beneficiaries age 65+ or disabled under 65 for QDWI.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Pays Part A/B premiums, deductibles, copays; QMB covers most out-of-pocket costs.[1][4]

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card for low-income individuals to buy healthy groceries. Helps combat food insecurity for seniors.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Rhode Islanders age 60+, with slightly adjusted eligibility for seniors/disabled.[5][6]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200+ monthly per household depending on size/income; no cooking requirement for seniors.[5]

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Assists with heating costs and energy bills for low-income households. Includes crisis assistance for emergencies.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled adults meeting income guidelines.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Grants up to $1,000+ annually for heating bills, weatherproofing, and emergencies.[4]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency in homes of low-income households through insulation, sealing, and efficiency upgrades. Reduces energy costs and improves comfort.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, disabled, and families in eligible RI homes.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Free home upgrades saving hundreds annually on energy bills.[4]

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free counseling on Medicare options, savings programs, and appeals. Helps seniors navigate coverage and costs.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Rhode Island Medicare beneficiaries, especially low-income seniors.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Personalized advice preventing costly mistakes; application assistance.[1]

programs.name: Meals on Wheels of Rhode Island
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Provides social contact during delivery.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound older adults unable to prepare meals, often low-income.[5]

programs.name: Caregiver/Respite Support
programs.what_it_does: Offers respite services and support for family caregivers through LTSS programs. Includes counseling and training via ADRC.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors/disabled adults needing relief.[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Paid respite hours, training, and support groups.[1]

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time paid training jobs in community service to low-income seniors. Builds skills for unsubsidized employment.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage training positions up to 20 hours/week.[6]

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal assistance for issues like benefits, housing, and abuse. Operated through elder advocacy networks.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Representation in benefits appeals, evictions, and elder rights cases.[1]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities resolving complaints and ensuring quality care. Protects rights of nursing home/assisted living residents.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors in RI nursing homes, assisted living, or similar facilities.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy, investigations, and systemic improvements.[1]

programs.name: Rhode Island Pharmaceutical Assistance for the Elderly (RIPAE)
programs.what_it_does: RIPAE provides financial assistance to eligible seniors for prescription drug costs not covered by Medicare or other insurance. It helps reduce out-of-pocket expenses for medications, supplementing federal programs like Medicare Part D Extra Help.
programs.who_its_for: Rhode Island residents aged 65+ with limited income and assets, who are enrolled in Medicare.
programs.whats_unique: State-funded pharmaceutical assistance program specific to RI, going beyond federal Medicare Part D by providing additional copayment coverage for seniors.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by income; covers prescription copays up to annual maximums (e.g., $1,500-$2,100 based on income levels)[7][5]

programs.name: At Home Cost Share Program
programs.what_it_does: This program offers case management and supportive services to help seniors remain safely in their homes. Participants contribute a cost-share based on income, with state funding covering the rest for services like personal care and homemaking.
programs.who_its_for: Rhode Island seniors aged 60+ who meet income guidelines (typically up to 225% FPL) and need assistance to avoid nursing home placement.
programs.whats_unique: RI state-funded program administered by Office of Healthy Aging, providing non-Medicaid home-based services with income-based cost-sharing unique to the state's aging services model.
programs.benefit_value: Up to $1,800 annually in services per participant, with cost-share from $0-$50/month depending on income[4][3]

programs.name: HCC Senior Companion Program
programs.what_it_does: Trained volunteers provide companionship, assistance with daily tasks, and socialization to isolated seniors in homes, adult day centers, or community sites. The program fosters independence and reduces loneliness through regular visits.
programs.who_its_for: Isolated Rhode Island seniors aged 60+ funded through RI Division of Elderly Affairs, regardless of income.
programs.whats_unique: State-funded volunteer companion service via RI Division of Elderly Affairs, emphasizing community-based support not universally replicated in other states.
programs.benefit_value: Free volunteer services averaging 20 hours/month per senior[3]

programs.name: The POINT
programs.what_it_does: The POINT is a statewide call center offering information, referrals, and assistance enrolling in long-term care options, Medicare, Medicaid, and other senior programs. It handles 40,000 calls yearly to connect seniors and caregivers to resources.
programs.who_its_for: Rhode Island seniors aged 60+, adults with disabilities, and caregivers seeking statewide aging services support.
programs.whats_unique: RI's centralized, multilingual Aging and Disability Resource Network (ADRN) hub operated by United Way, unique in its comprehensive statewide integration through 2-1-1.
programs.benefit_value: Free enrollment assistance into programs worth thousands in annual benefits per enrollee[1]

programs.name: Benefits Enrollment Center (BEC)
programs.what_it_does: BEC provides free enrollment help for RI Medicare beneficiaries into state and federal benefits programs. Counselors assist with applications for RIPAE, LIHEAP, SNAP, MSP, and LIS to maximize benefits.
programs.who_its_for: Rhode Island Medicare beneficiaries, especially seniors aged 65+, seeking benefits screening and application support.
programs.whats_unique: Statewide RI Office of Healthy Aging service focused on state-specific programs like RIPAE alongside federal ones, with dedicated phone appointments.
programs.benefit_value: Free service unlocking benefits averaging $5,000+ annually per enrollee (e.g., RIPAE, energy aid)[7]
